🎬↔📋 Protocol–Video Alignment — Claude vs VLM

A frozen snapshot of the live app: the SAME sampled frames + protocol reviewed by Claude · claude-opus-4-8 and a local open VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct), side by side, with a Claude↔VLM agreement strip. Task = serial dilution. See also the quantitative benchmark page.

1) Mark four sterile PCR tubes as 1, 10⁻¹ 10⁻² and 10⁻³.
2) Add 90 µL of original sample to the first tube. Add 90 µL of buffer to rest tubes.
3) Add 10 µL of the original sample into the 10⁻¹ tube and mix well by pipetting.
4) Transfer 10 µL from the 10⁻¹ tube into the 10⁻² tube, mix well. Repeat the process sequentially for 10⁻³ tubes.

✅ Serial dilution — “correct” run

Claude · claude-opus-4-8⏱ 22s
⚠ 3 steps worth a quick review
1. Mark four sterile PCR tubes as 1, 10⁻¹ 10⁻² and 10⁻³.⚠ review
⏱ 1m51s – 1m59s
💡 Labeling of the four tubes (1, 10⁻¹, 10⁻², 10⁻³) couldn't be clearly read; marker use is seen but worth confirming the correct labels.
A blue marker/Sharpie is handled and the worker appears to write on tubes (frames 14-15), but the specific dilution labels are not legible.
2. Add 90 µL of original sample to the first tube. Add 90 µL of buffer to rest tubes.⚠ review
⏱ 40s – 1m03s
💡 Couldn't confirm the 90 µL volumes or that sample vs. buffer went into the correct tubes — worth a quick look.
A PBS (buffer) conical tube is handled and liquid is dispensed into small tubes (frames 5-7), consistent with adding buffer, but exact volumes are not verifiable.
3. Add 10 µL of the original sample into the 10⁻¹ tube and mix well by pipetting.probably done
⏱ 1m03s – 1m27s
Worker pipettes small volumes into a held PCR tube using a small-volume micropipette (labeled ~20), consistent with a 10 µL addition and mixing.
4. Transfer 10 µL from the 10⁻¹ tube into the 10⁻² tube, mix well. Repeat the process sequentially for 10⁻³ tubes.⚠ review
⏱ 1m27s – 1m51s
💡 Sequential 10 µL transfers and mixing look to occur, but the order/correct tubes and tip changes between dilutions couldn't be fully confirmed — worth a glance.
Repeated pipetting/mixing between small tubes is seen (frames 11-13), with pinkish liquid visible during mixing in frame 13, consistent with serial transfers.
What it observed
A gloved worker operates inside a biosafety cabinet. The bench has a blue tube rack, a white rack, and a green PCR rack holding several small strip/PCR tubes, plus a NEST conical tube labeled 'PBS' (buffer), red-capped conical tubes, a Sharpie marker, and a stand of single-channel micropipettes. Across the clip the worker selects pipettes (frames 1,4,7,8), picks up the PBS tube (frames 5,7), and repeatedly holds small PCR tubes in one hand while pipetting liquid into/out of them with a micropipette (frames 6,9,10,11,12,13). In frame 13 a pinkish liquid is visible during mixing/transfer between tubes. Near the end (frames 14,15) the worker handles a Sharpie/marker and appears to write on the tubes. The overall sequence is consistent with preparing buffer-filled tubes and performing serial transfers, though exact volumes and labels are not legible.

What it observed
The clip is filmed inside a biosafety cabinet. Visible equipment includes a green PCR tube rack, a blue tube rack holding larger tubes (one with a blue/teal cap, plus red-capped conical tubes), a stand with several micropipettes, pipette tip boxes, reagent bottles, and a blue marker pen. A gloved operator (lab coat) handles micropipettes and small PCR/microtubes. Early frames (0-2) show setup and an initial pipetting action drawing from a blue-capped tube in the blue rack. Through frames 3-13 the operator repeatedly holds a small strip/tube in the left hand and pipettes into it with a single-channel pipette, consistent with sequential transfers between small tubes. A marker pen is present throughout but no clear labeling/writing action on tubes is captured. Frame 14 shows capping/handling of a small tube, and frame 15 shows the operator reaching toward the racks near the end.
